1: Chapter 22 continues the regulations for the Aaronic priesthood begun in chapter 21. Where chapter 21 addressed the personal holiness requirements of priests (physical wholeness, marriage rules, mourning prohibitions), chapter 22 addresses functional holiness: when priests may eat the sacred donations and what quality of animal is acceptable for sacrifice. The chapter belongs to the Holiness Code (Leviticus 17-26), which repeatedly grounds Israel's distinctiveness in YHWH's own holiness ('I am YHWH who sanctifies you,' Leviticus 22:32). The priestly regulations here are the internal counterpart to the general Israelite food and purity laws of chapters 11-15.
2: The command that priests 'keep away from the holy things' when impure protects the integrity of the sacred donations (qodashim). The priestly house received portions of the offerings — the terumah (heave offering), the breast and right thigh of peace offerings, the unleavened bread from Nazirite offerings, and portions of grain offerings. These donations sustained the priestly family economically in exchange for their tabernacle service. The system of sacred donations was also a form of social security for the priestly tribe, which owned no land (Numbers 18:20-24). Malachi 1:6-14 shows how the system was abused when priests accepted inferior animals and treated the sacred donations with contempt.
3: The karet (cutting off) penalty for eating holy food while ritually impure appears here as in other crucial holiness violations. In context, the priest who eats sacred food while impure has treated the holy as common — this is precisely the profanation (chillul) that the Holiness Code most consistently condemns. Second Temple priestly practice included elaborate procedures for confirming ritual purity before eating terumah: handwashing, immersion, and waiting periods were all part of maintaining the system the Torah requires.
4: The specific impurity conditions that disqualify a priest from eating sacred donations — skin disease (tzara'at), genital discharge (zob), corpse-contamination — are the same major categories addressed in chapters 13-15. The cross-referential structure is deliberate: the purity-law block and the priestly-regulation block are designed to be read together. A reader who has absorbed chapters 11-15 already knows exactly what conditions the priest must avoid and how to resolve each one.
5: Contact impurity transmission: touching someone who transmits corpse-impurity, or a person with a discharge, makes the priest impure. The impurity-chain logic (person A touched corpse → person B touched A → person B is now impure) works through physical contact. Numbers 19 develops the corpse-impurity rules in detail with the red heifer purification. The Second Temple period saw vigorous debate about the specifics of impurity transmission — the Pharisees and Sadducees disagreed on many points of application.
6: 'The person who touches any such thing shall be unclean until the evening and shall not eat of the holy things unless he has bathed his body in water.' The resolution mechanism for most minor impurities — water immersion + evening — was the standard baseline. Later Jewish practice formalized this as the mikveh (ritual immersion pool), which became a standard installation in any Jewish community. The evening boundary (when a new day begins in Jewish reckoning) created a clean daily reset for ordinary impurities.
7: 'When the sun goes down he shall be clean, and afterward he may eat of the holy things, for they are his food.' The priest's access to sacred donations is not a spiritual luxury but his sustenance — 'for they are his food.' The Levitical system created a labor-for-donations economy: priests served at the altar and in turn received portions of the offerings. Paul cites this principle directly: 'those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel' (1 Corinthians 9:13-14), explicitly drawing on the Levitical pattern.
8: Nevela (animal that died naturally) and trefah (animal torn by predators) are prohibited to priests — a stricter standard than for ordinary Israelites, who could give such meat to foreigners (Deuteronomy 14:21). The priestly diet was a subset of the broader Israelite dietary laws: what was permissible for the laity was not necessarily permissible for priests. This graduated holiness — ordinary Israelites, Levites, priests, high priest — each with progressively stricter purity requirements — reflects the concentric structure of the tabernacle itself (courts, holy place, holy of holies).
9: 'They shall therefore keep my charge, lest they bear sin for it and die thereby when they profane it.' The death penalty for priestly mishandling of holy things is not merely threatened but enacted in the narrative: Nadab and Abihu died for offering unauthorized fire (Leviticus 10:1-2); Uzzah died for touching the ark improperly (2 Samuel 6:7). These narratives function as cautionary examples that make the abstract warning of Leviticus 22:9 concrete. The priestly vocation was both privilege and mortal risk.
10: The household boundary for eating terumah: zar (stranger/outsider) cannot eat it. The 'stranger' here is not necessarily a non-Israelite but anyone not part of the priestly household by birth or purchase. A resident worker (toshav) or hired laborer (sakhir) employed by the priest — who lives in the house but is not a member of the family — cannot eat the sacred donations. The priestly household is a religious unit with economic boundaries as well as kinship ones.
11: A slave purchased by a priest becomes a full member of the priestly household and may eat sacred donations; a slave born in the priest's household has the same status. This is a significant statement: the purchased slave's belonging to the priestly family overrides his non-priestly origin. The principle that membership matters more than birth is embedded in the Levitical system. It contrasts with the daughter who married a non-priest (Leviticus 22:12) — marriage out of the priestly household removes access, while purchase into it grants access.
12: A priest's daughter who marries a non-priest (ish zar) loses access to the sacred donations. Her status follows her husband's household — she has become part of a non-priestly economic unit. This rule reflects the ancient Israelite household structure where a woman's status was determined by her husband's household. The loss of terumah access was a real economic consequence of marriage outside the priestly family, not merely a ritual technicality.
13: The divorced or widowed daughter who returns to her father's house without children — having no living descendants from the non-priestly marriage — regains access to sacred donations. The condition 'with no children' is key: if she has children, they belong to the non-priestly line and she is presumed to be supporting them, not fully re-integrated into the priestly household. This nuanced rule shows careful attention to the actual family economics of ancient Israel.
14: The accidental eating of sacred donations by a layman requires restoration at full value plus a 20% surcharge (chamishit). This parallels the guilt offering regulations (Leviticus 5:14-16): trespass against the holy — even unintentional — requires real restitution. The 20% premium marks the special status of what was encroached upon. Second Temple halakha developed detailed rules about when and how laypersons might accidentally encounter terumah and their corresponding obligations.
15: 'The priests shall not profane the holy things of the people of Israel, which they contribute to YHWH.' The priests bear responsibility not only for their own holiness but for protecting the integrity of the donation system on behalf of the whole people. If priests accept and eat donations improperly, they have profaned what the whole congregation brought to YHWH. This corporate responsibility of the priesthood is part of why the prophets reserved their sharpest critique for priestly corruption (Jeremiah 2:8; Ezekiel 22:26; Malachi 1:6-14).
16: 'Causing them to bear iniquity and guilt, by eating their holy things' — the priest's profanation becomes the people's guilt. The sacrificial system was a system of covenant mediation: when the mediating priests corrupted the system, the whole covenant community suffered the consequences. The prophetic warnings about priestly failure (especially Malachi) are grounded in this principle: Israel's relationship with YHWH is damaged by priestly mishandling of its sacred institutions.
17: The transition to offering regulations (Leviticus 22:17-25) addresses the quality of animals brought for burnt offerings and votive offerings. The neder (vow) and nedavah (freewill offering) represent two motivations for additional offerings beyond the required sacrifices: obligation fulfilled and spontaneous generosity. The tamim (perfect/without blemish) requirement applies to both — YHWH does not accept second-tier offerings regardless of the motivation behind them.
18: The law addresses offerings from 'the house of Israel or the sojourners in Israel' — resident non-Israelites could also bring offerings to YHWH's altar. This reflects the inclusive dimension of the tabernacle/temple as a place of access for all who aligned themselves with Israel's God. Numbers 15:15-16 makes this explicit: 'one statute for you and the stranger who sojourns among you.' The requirement that alien offerings also be tamim confirms that the standard transcends ethnic category.
19: 'For your offering to be accepted it shall be a male without blemish of the cattle or the sheep or the goats.' The three livestock categories — cattle, sheep, goats — comprise the whole of the Israelite domesticated herd. The male requirement for burnt and votive offerings (females were permitted for some sin offerings, Leviticus 4:28, 32) reflected both the economic logic of herd management and the symbolic association of the male with strength and completeness.
20: 'Anything that has a blemish, you shall not offer, for it will not be acceptable for you.' The principle of acceptability (ratzon) is the governing criterion: YHWH's pleasure is not a given but depends on the integrity of the offering. Malachi 1:8 applies this directly: 'When you offer blind animals in sacrifice, is that not evil? And when you offer those that are lame or sick, is that not evil? Present that to your governor; will he accept you or show you favor?' The prophetic critique translates the Levitical standard into political-relational terms.
21: The peace offering (shelamim) for a vow or freewill offering must be tamim with no blemish. The shelamim's communal character — portions to YHWH, priests, and offerer — meant that a blemished animal would have been consumed by the priestly family. The law protects the integrity of what the priests receive as well as what YHWH 'receives' (consumes in the altar fire). The whole offering economy depended on the tamim standard being maintained at every point.
22: The catalog of disqualifying conditions — blind, injured, maimed, with running sores, scabs, or scurvy — covers visible physical defects. The comprehensiveness of the list is designed to close any possible loophole for priestly discretion in accepting marginal animals. Compare with the Second Temple practice: the Mishnah (tractate Bekhorot) devotes extensive chapters to exactly which physical conditions constitute blemishes and which are within normal variation. The Levitical list generated centuries of legal interpretation.
23: A freewill offering (nedavah) may be an ox or sheep with a limb too long or too short — a minor asymmetry that would disqualify for vowed offerings but is acceptable for the spontaneous offering. This distinction between vow fulfillment and freewill offering shows that the same blemish level does not apply equally to all offering types. The freewill offering's lower standard reflects its character as a gift from the heart; the vow fulfillment's stricter standard reflects that a made commitment must be fully honored.
24: The prohibition on offering bruised, crushed, torn, or cut animals extends the blemish rules to encompass human-caused alterations. Castration of animals was practiced in surrounding cultures for various agricultural purposes. The prohibition here ensures that the offering presented to YHWH has not been altered from its natural created state — it must be as it came from the Creator's hand. The same principle of natural integrity underlies the tamim requirement generally.
25: 'Neither shall you offer as the bread of your God any such animals bought from a foreigner. Since there is a blemish in them, a defect in them, they will not be accepted for you.' Even purchased foreign animals must meet the blemish-free standard. This confirms that the acceptability criterion is intrinsic to the animal, not to its source. The phrase 'bread of your God' (lechem Elohekhem) applies the offering vocabulary of the grain offering to animals, framing all altar offerings as YHWH's food — a common ancient Near Eastern conceptual framework that Leviticus employs while simultaneously differentiating YHWH from gods who 'need' food.
26: Newborn animals must remain with their mothers for seven days before being eligible for offering. The seven-day qualification period may reflect the natural bonding period observed between mother and offspring. It also parallels the human circumcision timing (eight days after birth) and the Sabbath pattern — the first seven-day cycle completes before the animal enters the world of religious obligation. This natural qualification period resists the pressure to offer immediately (perhaps from newly born animals given as freewill offerings) in favor of the rhythms of creation.
27: The law of mother-and-young not to be slaughtered on the same day (Leviticus 22:28) is here introduced with the seven-day waiting period for newborns. Both laws protect the natural mother-offspring relationship. Deuteronomy 22:6-7 (the bird's nest law) is a parallel: the mother bird must not be taken with the eggs. Both laws express a reverence for natural bonds that even the slaughter system must not gratuitously violate. The Mishnaic tractate Chullin and later responsa literature discuss these laws extensively.
28: 'But you shall not kill an ox or a sheep and her young in one day.' The prohibition on same-day killing of mother and offspring is one of a handful of laws that recognize animal relationships in Levitical ethics. It is not anthropomorphism but a practical expression of the principle that creation's order is not to be carelessly disrupted. Later Jewish ethics (tzaar ba'alei chayyim, the prohibition on causing unnecessary animal suffering) grounds itself partly in this verse.
29: The thanksgiving offering (toda) regulations: it must be eaten on the day of sacrifice with nothing left until morning (Leviticus 22:30). The toda was the most situationally specific of the peace offerings — brought in response to a particular act of divine deliverance (Psalms 107:22). Psalms 50:14-15 and 23 identify the toda as the offering YHWH most desires: 'offer to God a sacrifice of thanksgiving; call upon me in the day of trouble; I will deliver you, and you shall glorify me.' The same-day eating rule creates urgency and prevents the thanksgiving from being deferred or commercialized.
30: The same-day rule for the toda distinguishes it from the regular peace offering (which could be eaten over two days, Leviticus 7:15-16) and the freewill/vow offerings (which allowed a second day). The theological point: the thanksgiving must be consumed in the immediate context of the gratitude that prompted it. Gratitude has a shelf life; the law builds in the pastoral wisdom that acts of thanksgiving should be expressed while the sense of divine help is fresh and vivid.
31: 'So you shall keep my commandments and do them: I am YHWH.' The dual verb shamar (keep/guard) and asah (do/perform) is the Holiness Code's standard covenant-obedience formula. Keeping means internalizing and guarding; doing means acting. The formula grounds all the specific regulations in the direct authority of YHWH's self-identification. The repeated 'I am YHWH' throughout chapters 18-26 (occurring over 40 times) functions as the non-negotiable authorization behind each law.
32: 'And you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified among the people of Israel.' The concept of kiddush hashem (sanctifying the Name) and chillul hashem (profaning the Name) — articulated here more clearly than anywhere else in the Torah — became foundational for Jewish ethics. YHWH's reputation among Israel and the nations is a stake in every act of covenant faithfulness or unfaithfulness. Ezekiel develops this extensively (Ezekiel 36:20-23): Israel's exile profaned YHWH's name among the nations; the new covenant restoration will vindicate it. The concept underlies Paul's lament in Romans 2:24 ('the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you') and the Lord's Prayer ('hallowed be your name').
33: 'I am YHWH who sanctifies you, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God: I am YHWH.' The closing formula anchors the holiness requirements in the Exodus narrative. Israel's distinctive practices are the response to YHWH's prior saving act — not the condition of it. The sequence is always redemption first (Exod 1-18), then covenant obligation (Exod 19-Lev). This Exodus-grounding of ethics appears across the Torah (Exodus 20:2 precedes the Decalogue; Deuteronomy 6:21-25 connects commandments to the Exodus) and is fundamental to understanding how Levitical holiness works.
